coredata.dc:title: Microfluidic-based fluorescence enhancement of silica-embedded carbon dots for direct detection and quantification of unamplified HCV RNA in clinical samples

coredata.dc:description:
Background Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) is an asymptomatic chronic infection with serious clinical consequences. Timely and sensitive detection of HCV RNA is critical for infection control, treatment response follow up. While current technologies, such as PCR or isothermal amplification-based strategies, are specific, they are expensive, labor intensive, time consuming limiting their use in field and small laboratories.
Results We introduce a novel technology for detecting nucleic acids, as exemplified by HCV RNA, in clinical specimens. This approach utilizes the crosslinked Enhanced Emission (CEE) phenomena upon mixing fluorescent amino functionalized silica-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs/SiO2/NH2) with magnetic extracted unamplified HCV RNA showed a significant and immediate fluorescence enhancement. This method was integrated into a semi-automated 3D printed microfluidic chip, wherein the unamplified RNA was mixed with the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2. The assay was also employed on the conventional 96-well plate format. This assay offers high sensitivity with detection limit of 500 IU/ml and 1000 IU/ml for the chip and well plate respectively. The sample-to-result time was <20 min on the chip and is simpler than the amplification-based techniques. Analyzing 141 patient samples yielded sensitivity and specificity of 96.47 % and 98.79 % respectively.
Significance This application of N-CDs/SiO2 as nucleic acid fluorescent probes for the first time, offers a versatile, cost-effective, and universal platform for nucleic acids detection. The developed system can be employed in conventional microwell plate-based detection with minimal modifications to current laboratory setups. Additionally, it was integrated into a 3D-printed microfluidic chip enabling enhancement in specificity, sensitivity and accuracy.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Emission spectra at excitation/emission (355 and 520 nm) respectively of bare N-CDs/SiO2 and N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 nanoparticles, with and without HCV RNA of different concentrations, showing significant enhancing fluorescence, (b) The fluorescence enhancement of different RNA concentrations related to the N-CDs/SiO2 and N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 nanoparticles.

(a) FTIR spectra of (curve a) TMA+/SiO2 before calcination, (curve b) SiO2/CDs after calcination of TMA/SiO2, (curve c) after surface modification with APTES (APTES/SiO2/CDs), (b1) zeta potential of CDs/SiO2 (−33.3 ± 2 mv), (b2) zeta potential of CSs/SiO2/NH2 (13.7 ± 2 mv).
(a) FTIR spectrum for MBs/SiO2–NH2 (curve a) MBs, curve (b) MBs/SiO2, curve (c) MBs/SiO2/NH2, (b1) zeta potential for MBs/SiO2 (−27.8 ± 2), (b2) zeta potential for MBs/SiO2–NH2 (17.5 ± 2 mv).

Proposed fluorometric assay for determining HCV viral RNA concentration (IU/ml) and percentage of fluorescence enhancement for N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 at excitation/emission wavelengths of 355/520 nm. This includes (a) Dynamic range and standard curve on the microwell plate platform, and (b) Dynamic range and standard curve on the microfluidic chip platform. As shown in both figures, the fluorescent enhancement in the microfluidic chip is higher than the microwell, with just a quarter of the assay volume, which enhances the overall assay performance.

(a) and (b) ROC curves illustrating assay cutoff, specificity, and sensitivity for HCV viral RNA detection across 141 samples tested on both the microwell plate and microfluidic chip platforms with P-value <0.0001 flagged with ∗∗∗∗, (c) Box-whisker plots indicate the nonparametric Kruskal-Wallis test assessing the similarity of HCV viral load results between both platforms of Nano-assay and qPCR. and (d) Positive Spearman correlation coefficient (r) demonstrating the relationship between HCV viral load measured by the nano-assay on both platforms (microwell (assay MW.), Microfluidic (assay MF.), and qPCR over patient samples.

Reproducibility of Nano assay on both microwell platform and microfluidic chip respectively, inter-assay (a) and (c) for 3 different positive samples measured over three plates and chips represented by (T = test), (b) and (d) intra-assay over a duplicate of three different positive samples with different concentrations (S=sample); S1(4266 IU/ml), S2 (634,717 IU/ml), S3 (1,464,746 IU/ml). Error bars represent SD across triplicates (n = 3) (e) Coefficient variation of inter and intra Nano assay on both platforms, showing higher analytical performance for the microfluidic chip than the microwell.

(a)AFM image of N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 after incubation with HCV-negative sample and (b) HCV- positive sample. As shown a significant difference in height between the positive and negative samples confirms the resulting clustering structure due to RNA/N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 interaction. (c)Fluorescent microscope images of N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 after incubation with an HCV-negative sample and (d) with an HCV-positive sample supporting the aggregated/clustering structure (e) Time-resolved fluorescence decay shows a lifetime of the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 in HCV positive sample and negative control sample.

Schematic depiction of the assay procedure for the microfluidic-based platform: HCV RNA is captured from samples using capture probe-functionalized MBs. After washing, the captured RNA is heat eluted, then transferred to the microfluidic chip, where it is mixed with N-CDs/SiO2/NH2/PBS and delivered to a sample well. The fluorescence is measured using a conventional plate reader and compared to a control well that has blank N-CDs/SiO2/NH2. All dimensions of the microfluidic chip are in mm.
Comparative analysis of selected biosensors for HCV RNA detection and quantification.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) is an asymptomatic chronic infection with serious clinical consequences. Timely and sensitive detection of HCV RNA is critical for infection control, treatment response follow up. While current technologies, such as PCR or isothermal amplification-based strategies, are specific, they are expensive, labor intensive, time consuming limiting their use in field and small laboratories.

We introduce a novel technology for detecting nucleic acids, as exemplified by HCV RNA, in clinical specimens. This approach utilizes the crosslinked Enhanced Emission (CEE) phenomena upon mixing fluorescent amino functionalized silica-coated nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs/SiO2/NH2) with magnetic extracted unamplified HCV RNA showed a significant and immediate fluorescence enhancement. This method was integrated into a semi-automated 3D printed microfluidic chip, wherein the unamplified RNA was mixed with the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2. The assay was also employed on the conventional 96-well plate format. This assay offers high sensitivity with detection limit of 500 IU/ml and 1000 IU/ml for the chip and well plate respectively. The sample-to-result time was <20 min on the chip and is simpler than the amplification-based techniques. Analyzing 141 patient samples yielded sensitivity and specificity of 96.47 % and 98.79 % respectively.

This application of N-CDs/SiO2 as nucleic acid fluorescent probes for the first time, offers a versatile, cost-effective, and universal platform for nucleic acids detection. The developed system can be employed in conventional microwell plate-based detection with minimal modifications to current laboratory setups. Additionally, it was integrated into a 3D-printed microfluidic chip enabling enhancement in specificity, sensitivity and accuracy.

A microfluidic system developed to enable fast mixing between Isolated RNA and nitrogen dopped carbon dots embedded within silica nanoparticles (N-CDs/SiO2). The interaction between the RNA and N-CDs/SiO2 result in a concentration-dependent fluorescence enhancement.
Image 1

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Introduction
ce:section.ce:para: Hepatitis C Virus (HCV) infection poses a significant worldwide public health challenge, with approximately 58 million people living with chronic HCV infection, and 1.5 million new infections occurring each year [1]. Direct-acting antivirals (DAA), typically administered over a span of 8–12 weeks for HCV infection management [2]. Real-time polymerase Chain Reaction (qPCR) is still the technique of choice for the detection and follow-up of treatment response to follow up the response of treatment. However, qPCR suffers from its high cost, labor-intensive nature, and the need for sophisticated equipment [3]. On the other hand, HCV serological testing is unable to follow up treatment for its incompetence to detect neither active infection nor treatment response i.e. viral RNA [3].

World Health Organization's is aiming of reducing global new infections by 90 % and mortality rates related to viral hepatitis by 65 % by 2030 [4]. Creating an affordable, rapid, sensitive, and user-friendly HCV biosensor is the main pillar of WHO to achieve its goals [5]. Despite the emergence of Various HCV RNA biosensors, that overcome some limitations of qPCR [5–7], they always require nucleic acid (RNA) amplification, or enzyme-based signal amplification steps extending the assay time, and complexity [3,8].

Carbon dots (CDs) are amorphous fluorescent nanoparticles comprising sp2/sp3 carbon network, with unique optical properties affected by surface and composition core state [9]. Improving the optical activity of CDs is attained by heteroatom doping, including nitrogen, boron, and sulfur. Among these, nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDs) are known to modulate and enhance CDs luminescent performance and efficiency [10]. However, like many other types of carbon dots, N-CDs also encounter the issue of self-aggregation leading to self-quenching [11,12]. Coating CDs with a safeguarding matrix shell, such as silica, can significantly enhance their stability and biocompatibility while preserving their optical properties [11,13].

Cross-linking Enhanced Emission (CEE) phenomena leads to improvement of the CDs emission intensity, through the formation of covalent bonding or noncovalent interactions such as hydrogen bonding, π-π stacking, van der Waals forces, and ionic bonding and subsequent enhancement of CDs luminescence [14,15]. This is achieved by the formation of a more rigid structure, and stable cross-linked network around the core of CDs, that minimize their non-radiative decay pathways, enhancing fluorescence intensity and stability [16,17]; which are required in the vast majority of applications such as sensing, bioimaging, and photonic devices [18].

Traditional plate-based or test tube-based strategies employing fluorescent sensors have been widely applied in a variety of sensing applications, including the detection of proteins, nucleic acids, and small molecules. However, integrating these fluorescent sensors within microfluidic platforms offers several distinct advantages. Microfluidics can significantly reduce the required sample volume, minimize variability, and facilitate automation, leading to more consistent and reliable results [19]. Although simple serpentine mixers may not be highly sophisticated, they provide effective solutions for miniaturizing processes while precisely handling smaller volumes of samples and reagents. These mixers enable proper and efficient mixing of specimens and reagents, which is crucial for optimizing reactions within the microfluidic system. These have been integrated with an array of signal transduction strategies such as fluorescence [20], and electrochemical [21,22], achieving remarkable specificities and sensitivities.

Herein, we employed a novel approach for quantifying RNA exploiting the CEE of fluorescent in-house synthesized silica-embedded nitrogen-doped carbon dots (N-CDS/SiO2) upon interaction with HCV RNA. The assay was integrated within a simple 3D printed microfluidic chip featuring a serpentine mixer to precisely control sample and reagent mixing, minimizing sample volumes, and reducing user-related variabilities, enabling the direct detection of unamplified HCV RNA in clinical samples. HCV RNA specifically extracted on functionalized magnetic beads is mixed with positively charged N-CDs/SiO2/NH2, and fluorescence intensity is measured within 5 min (Scheme 1 ). This detection method was successfully applied in conventional microwell plate and microfluidic chip, underpinning its potential applications and increased versatility.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Materials and methods
Chemicals and equipment
ce:section.ce:para: The equipment and chemicals used in this study can be found in detail in supplementary materials.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Serum samples collection
ce:section.ce:para: The current study was approved by Ain Shams ethical committee, Faculty of Medicine. The Federal-wide Assurance (FWA- 000017585) and collected following the ethical approval and informed consent of all subjects. Total sample count was 141 samples, with HCV-positive serum (n = 108), including 85 samples with chronic HCV infection and 23 post-treated HCV infections, In addition to, 33 specimens of health volunteers. All the samples were subjected to rapid and ELISA for HCV Abs, and Hepatitis B virus surface antigen (HBs Ag). Pooled negative serum samples were pooled as a negative control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, amino functionalization, and characterization of N-Dopped carbon dots embedded in Silica nanoparticles
ce:section.ce:para:
N-dopped carbon dots embedded in Silica nanoparticles (N-CDs/SiO
2) were synthesized as mentioned elsewhere with slight modifications [23]. Briefly, 500 ml of ethanol was mixed with 10 ml of ultrapure water & then, incubated in a water bath at 30 °C for 30 min. Subsequently, 10 ml of 15 % wt./wt. In water, TMAOH and 10 ml of TEOS were added to the water/ethanol mixture under stirring at 600 RPM for 3 h. Then, centrifuged at 12,000 RPM for 30 min followed by multiple washing of water and ethanol, and the precipitate was dried at 60 °C under vacuum for 12 h to obtain TMA
+
/SiO
2 particles and then, calcined at 300 °C for 3 h under vacuum to form the N-CDs/SiO
2 .

Three hundred mg of the as-synthesized N-CD
s
/SiO
2 , was sonicated in 100 mL 70 % ethanol till full dispersion, followed by the addition of 2 ml Amino Propyl triethoxysilane silane (APTES) for amino functionalization under stirring for 3 h at 70 °C. Then, centrifuged at 12,000 RPM for 20 min, washed multiple times with water/ethanol, then dried at 60 °C for 2 h. The TMA
+
/SiO
2 , was characterized with FTIR, while N-CD
s
/SiO
2, and amino-modified N-CDs silica (N-CD
s
/SiO
2
/NH
2
) were characterized by fluorimeter, XRD, XPS, SEM, Zeta Sizer, and potential, and FTIR using KBr disc method.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, functionalization, and characterization of amine-functionalized, silica-coated magnetic beads (MB/Si/NH2)
ce:section.ce:para: Magnetic beads (Fe3O4), synthesis, silica coating, and amino functionalization (MB/Si/NH2) have been performed as described elsewhere [24]. The synthesized MB/Si/NH2 were characterized by the FTIR KBr disc method, zeta sizer, and potential, while the amino groups were quantified by amino colloidal titration using polyvinyl sulfate and toluidine blue [25]. The detailed procedures for synthesis and functionalization will be found in the supplementary document.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of (MBs/SiO2/NH2) with HCV specific target-probe (HCV nanoprobe)
ce:section.ce:para: The thiolated HCV-specific probe was conjugated to the MBs/SiO2/NH2 through bifunctional group crosslinker 3-maleimidobenzoic acid N-hydroxyl succinimide (MBS) under specific conditions and buffers as mentioned elsewhere [26,27]. The number of probes attached to the particles has been measured using the Beer-Lambert law from their absorbance at 260 nm [28].

ce:section.ce:section-title: HCV RNA extraction from serum samples
Designing and 3D printing of the microfluidic chip
ce:section.ce:para: The microfluidic chip was designed using Fusion 360© (Autodesk, USA) software based on predetermined design parameters, which are microchannel dimensions, shape, configuration of the microchannels, and the associated functionalization areas (reagent chambers and detection microwells), detection method, number of reagents needed to perform the assay, material, and printer used for 3D printing. The corrected and finalized design was established according to the assay requirements, and then it was exported to the slicing software (PreForm-3), as depicted in supplementary (Fig. 2S). The designed chip, with dimensions explicitly described in Scheme 1, was then printed using the 3D Form lab B+ Printer (Fig. 3S). The printed chips were washed (sonicated × 2 in isopropanol) and then cured using Form Cure (Form lab UV Curing station) at 60 °C for 30 min [29]. The chip encompasses two inlets that connect to a serpentine mixer with an outlet that can be switched between waste and a fluorescence detection compartment. This compartment is designed to mimic the dimensions of a standard microwell with an adjacent well of the same dimensions used for negative control (Fig. 4S).
Supplementary video related to this article can be found at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.aca.2024.343396

The following is/are the supplementary data related to this article:
Multimedia component 2 Multimedia component 2

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microfluidic system establishment and optimization
ce:section.ce:para:
The OB1 MK4 Elvis flow system (Elveflow©, France) was assembled following company's instructions and has been used for automated fluids handling (samples and reagent delivery), following the company-recommended protocol and setup (Fig. 5S). The Jun-Air compressor (part 1) served as the air pressure source, connected to a filter cap for air dryer and particle filtration, entering the OB1 MK4 and allowing to set the output pressure as low as 1.5 mbar. Connecting the OB1 MK4 Elvis flow (part 2) to the Jun-Air compressor with an output value adjusted to at least 2.5 bars. ESI software (part 3) integration of OB1-MK4 with 2 channels and was configured to a pressure range of 0–200 mbar, and 0–2000 mbar for channels 1 and 2 respectively. Each channel was linked to a sensor connector and a pressure outlet, connected to the MFS-4 (Microfluidics flow rate sensor 40–1000 μl/min) (part 5) and the reservoir tube inlet (part 4), respectively, as depicted in Fig. 5S. The outlet microfluidic tubing of the reservoir was connected to the MFS-4. A controlled liquid flow rate was achieved by the MFS-4, with a specific resistance tube used for each channel for precise flow control and to maintain stable pressure (part 6), and the outlet tubing of the resistance tube was connected to the microfluidic chip, as shown in (part 7). The flow control method entailed pressurizing the liquid reservoir using a pressure controller, as depicted in Fig. 5S (part 4), that creates a pressure gradient between the inlet and outlet (ΔP) facilitating smooth and rapid liquid flow to the microfluidic chip, in which the flow was controlled by regulating the input gas pressure of the tank, as the flow rate (Q) is directly proportionally and inversely proportional to (ΔP) and resistance (R) respectively
Δ P = Q × R
.

Colored solutions were first used for the optimization of the chambers ability to hold the intended solution volumes and to assess the smooth flow and mixing of solutions under optimized pressure. A highly tidy process was followed to optimize the pressure-driven flow controller over the microfluidic chip to achieve equal flow rate (Q) over the 2 inlet channels of the used chip. The MF chip has two inlet chambers (one for the sample and the other for the reagent) connected with two inlet channels, each connected to OB1 Elvis's flow channels 1 and 2, respectively. The successful flow rate was achieved at 50 μl/min combined flow rate channels, by adjusting 20 mbar for channel 1 (with resistance tube diameter = 250 μm) and 80 mbar for channel 2 (connected to resistance tube 175 μm). They were then mixed in the serpentine mixing area at a 50 μl/min flow rate for 3 min; to reach the collected chamber and then passed to the measuring well within 1 min. The use of colored solutions helps in observing and detecting flow rates and solution mixing as demonstrated in (Fig. 4S) and video 1 (showing the mixing process within the designed microfluidic chip.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of HCV RNA using the developed assay (Micro well and microfluidic chip platforms)
ce:section.ce:para: The assay was performed on two platforms i) a traditional 96- microwell plate and the designed ii) microfluidic chip. In the microwell platform 10 μl of RNA was mixed with 150 μl (3 mg/ml) of N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 in RNase-free water and 40 μl PBS (pH 7.4) and reading was obtained in 5 min using a microplate reader with excitation/emission wavelength (355/520 nm).

On the other hand, the designed microfluidic chip platform, the samples, and the reagents delivery were automated using an OB1 MK4 Elvis flow system (Elveflow©, France). The N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 (40 μl) were mixed with (10 μl) PBS and (40 μL H2O + 10 μL RNA) were introduced separately into two inlet chip channels, each connected to OB1 Elvis's flow channels 1 and 2, respectively. They were then mixed in the serpentine mixing area at a 50 μl/min flow rate using the OB1 Elvis Flow controller. Following the mixing process, which typically takes around 3 min, the mixture is collected in the mixing chamber (100 μl). Then 50 μl is transferred through microfluidic tubing at the same pressure detection microwell. The microfluidic chip fluorescence measurement was achieved by sticking the chip with double-face tape to a 96-well plate cover, and fluorescence reading was obtained by the plate reader using the above-mentioned excitation/emission wavelengths. All the samples were tested in the presence of the pooled negative HCV samples as a negative control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of HCV viral load using real-time PCR and the developed assay
ce:section.ce:para: According to the manufacturer manual, HCV RNA viral load was quantified using an Artus Qiagen HCV real-time RT-PCR kit. On the other hand, quantification of all the samples using the developed assay has been performed by plotting the standard curve of the fluorescence enhancement percentage (Fl. E. %) as a y-axis against log concentration of HCV RNA serial dilutions, and the results were expressed in IU/ml. Specificity, sensitivity, and cut-off value has been determined by the receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curves, using GraphPad Prism 8.1 [30].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Statistical analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Statistical analysis was performed using GraphPad Prism 8.1 [30]. Continuous, normally distributed variables were expressed as mean ± SD with a 95 % confidence interval (CI); non-normally distributed variables were described as the median and interquartile range (IQR). P-value less than 0.05 was considered statistically significant. One-way ANOVA compared the means of normally distributed variables between independent groups, while the Kruskal-Wallis test and Spearman correlation coefficient were used for non-normally distributed variables.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Result and discussion
N-dopped carbon dots embedded in Silica nanoparticles (N-CDs/SiO2)
ce:section.ce:para: TMA+ acts as a direct catalyst for hydrolysis/condensation of TEOS and growth of silica nanoparticles producing (SiO2/TMA+) which, then acted as a precursor for in situ generation of N-CDs after calcination at 300 °C for 3 h. The luminescent properties of the silica particles enclosing N-CDs were investigated to further prove the formation of N-CDs after calcination, along with three positive samples of different concentrations (Fig. 1 a and b). The FTIR analysis shown in (Fig. 2 a) of (SiO2/TMA+, N-CDs/SiO2, and N-CDs/SiO2/NH2) revealed a band at 1480 cm−1 that assigned to the bending vibration of N+―OH- of SiO2/TMA+ (curve a), which disappeared after calcination suggesting the complete decomposition of TMA+ upon calcination and formation of N-CDs (curve b). The bands at 794, 1094, and 461 cm_1 represent the un-symmetric and symmetric linear stretching and the bending vibration peaks of Si–O–Si. These confirm the formation of silica matrices around CDs. The free amino groups' N–H bending and stretching vibration is shown at 1632 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1, respectively, with the characteristic fork-shaped band of free amino at 3500 cm−1(curve c). The anti-symmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations band of the propyl group appeared at 2921 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1 respectively (curve c), which confirms the anchored propyl group of APTES. The surface functionalization was further confirmed by the change in the zeta potential of N/CDs/SiO2 before and after functionalization with APTES as demonstrated in (Fig. 2, b1 and b2), where the mean zeta potential changes from negative (−33.3 ± 2mv) to positive (13.7 ± 2 mv) due to the formation of primary amine functional groups on the surface of the SiO2.

SEM coupled to EDX further confirmed the morphology and structure of the as-synthesized particles (Fig. 3 a and c) with a mean diameter of 400 (±50) nm for the N-CDs/SiO2 as further confirmed by TEM (Fig. 3b). XRD pattern centered at 2ᶿ = 23.7 indicating the amorphous characters of the N-CDs/SiO2 (Fig. 3d). High-resolution XPS of N-CDs/SiO2 also confirmed their structure as the particles presented 4 typical peaks C1s (286.49 eV), N1s (402.03veV), O1s (533.92 eV), and Si2p (104.39 eV) as shown in (Fig. 3e). The deconvolution of the XPS produced peaks is discussed in more details in supplementary material (see SI,
Fig. 6S
for more information). The emission wavelength of the formed N-CDs depends on the amount of TEOS, TMAOH (15 %), the calcination time, and temperature, which produces green emission of the embedded N-CDs within the amorphous Silica oxide (SiO2), which prevents self-quenching of the embedded N-CDs [14,23].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis, functionalization, and characterization of magnetic silica amino beads (MP/Si/NH2)
ce:section.ce:para:
The as-synthesized size of MBs/SiO2 particles was confirmed by a zeta sizer with an average size of 1454 ± 242 nm (Fig. 1S). FTIR spectra confirmed the surface modifications of MBs with silica and the primary amine groups. As shown in (Fig. 4 a), the band at 583 cm−1 reveals Fe–O stretching vibration of magnetic particles (curve a). The unsymmetric and symmetric linear stretching vibrations of Si–O–Si bonding are shown at bands 805 and 1084 cm−1, respectively; while bending vibration absorption peaks of Si–O–Si and Si–OH were observed at 461 cm−1 and 962 cm−1, respectively confirming the coating of MBs with a silica layer (MBs/SiO2, curve b). The N–H bending and stretching vibrations of the free amino groups are shown at 1632 cm−1 and 3500 cm−1, respectively with the characteristic fork-shaped band of free amino at 3500 cm−1 (curve c). The anti-symmetric and symmetric C–H stretching vibrations band of the propyl (alkane) group appeared at 2921 cm−1 and 2855 cm−1, respectively, and the bending vibration absorption peaks of -CH2 and -CH3 appeared at 1473 cm−1 and 1386 cm−1, respectively. These indicated the successful introduction of free amine groups through the APTES modification of the surface of MBs/SiO2. The mean zeta potential changes from negative (−27.8 ± 2) to positive values (17.5 ± 2 mv) due to the formation of primary amine functional groups on the surface of the MBs/SiO2 after functionalization with APTES, (Fig. 4 b1 & b2). Colloidal titration by polyvinyl sulfate using toluidine blue as indicator revealed 2.4 × 10−7 mol of amino groups per 1 mg particles. The size distribution of MBs/SiO
2
/NH
2 was (1454 ± 242 nm) as measured by the zeta sizer and shown in Fig. 1S.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Functionalization of MBs/SiO2/NH2 with HCV-specific target probes for (HCV nanoprobe)
ce:section.ce:para: Absorption measurements at 260 nm before and after conjugation have revealed about 110 pmol of HCV-specific probe per 1 mg of MBs/SiO2/NH2 (HCV nanoprobe), which is then used for RNA extraction directly from serum.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Quantification of HCV RNA concentration using the proposed assay with unlabeled N-CDs/SiO2/NH2
ce:section.ce:para:
Eluted RNA (positive & negative) was mixed with the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 and PBS 1X, and fluorescence was measured. The presence of HCV RNA has significantly enhanced the fluorescence intensity compared to the negative samples, with a strong linear correlation with the RNA concentration. The percentage of fluorescence intensity enhancement (FI. E.%) for N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 was calculated as follows:
Equation 1
F I .
( E
.
%
)
=
(
Sample
FI − Control
FI
Control
FI
)
× 100

Standard curves for the developed assay were generated by plotting (FI. E. %) against the log concentration of viral RNA. Both platforms (microwell plate and microfluidic chip) exhibited a strong linear correlation in the standard curves as shown in (Fig. 5 a and b), with R2 = 0.9878 and 0.9912 respectively, from which the viral loads of all samples were calculated. The fluorescence intensity plateau has been reached at about 1.7
×
106 IU/ml. Thus, higher concentrations were diluted to fit within the linear dynamic range.

The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve of the microwell plate (Fig. 6 a) has shown a specificity of (94.64 %) and sensitivity of (91.76 %), with a positive predictive value (PPV) of 96.3 % and a negative predictive value (NPV) of 88.3 %. The area under the curve (AUC) was 0.9585, and the overall accuracy was 93.79 % (P < 0.0001), with a cutoff value of 1.7 % fluorescence enhancement, and a detection limit of 1000 IU/ml.

The receiver operating characteristic (ROC) curve, of the microfluidic chip platform (Fig. 6 b), has shown higher specificity (98.21 %) and sensitivity (96.47 %), with a positive predictive value (PPV) of 98.79 % and a negative predictive value (NPV) of 96.4 %. The area under the curve (AUC) was 0.9676, and the overall accuracy was 97.3 % (P < 0.0001), with a cutoff value of 1.5 % fluorescence enhancement, and a detection limit with a detection limit of 500 IU/ml.

There is a highly significant positive correlation between the qPCR and the nano-assay with (p < 0.0001) and Spearman's correlation coefficient (r = 0.9655 and 0.9838) respectively, as shown in (Fig. 6d). The Kruskal-Wallis test indicated no statistically significant difference between the HCV viral load results obtained by the nano-assay (on both platforms) and qPCR, with a p-value of 0.9891, as shown in (Fig. 6c).

The Nano assay showed high inter and intra-day reproducibility over both platforms while the microfluidic has a higher performance (Fig. 7 a, b, c, and d). The coefficient of variation (COV) was <10 % across 3 replicates for both assays as shown in (Fig. 7e). Microwell inter and intraday (7.49 and 5.9) and microfluidic (5.21 and 3.54) respectively. Despite the data for both platforms are acceptable, the microfluidic chip has shown higher analytical performance than the microwell, which is attributed to several factors, most notably, sample/reagents proper handling, the precise control of pressure (pressure field driven micromixer), flow rate and volume provided by the microfluidic platform, along with the chaotic advection enabled by the serpentine mixer design, and the small volume (quarter of the reagent volume) which cannot be replicated/replaced by simple tubing and junctions that require larger volumes [31–33].

In the same context, the microfluidic platform showed a clear significantly higher fluorescence intensity (FI) enhancement compared to the microwell platform in differentiating/resolving between samples with close concentrations, especially at lower viral loads (SI Fig. 10S), thereby enhancing the overall sensitivity, specificity, accuracy, and reproducibility of the assay [34], with advantages of straightforward application of microfluidics [35], and overcome the challenging lengthy and costly processes required for RNA detection [36–38]. This has clinical significance in follow-up treatment response, applications in blood banks, and emergency cases.

Moreover, microfluidic chips offer several advantages, including an increased surface area to volume ratio, potential for automation, shorter assay times, and reduced background signal, primarily due to efficient mixing, that minimizes reading variations of the same sample and ensures consistent reagent distribution.

The microfluidic-based assay demonstrated slightly better performance compared to the microwell-based assay, using only a quarter of the reagent volume required for the microwell system, indicating the flexibility and feasibility of the assay to be used with different reading out.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Investigation of the RNA and N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 complex emission enhancement
ce:section.ce:para:
In this study, we have produced cationic N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 that are photostable, at various temperatures, pHs with the elimination of aggregation-caused quenching (AGQ) [23]; which have been exploited for the direct detection and quantification of unamplified HCV RNA in clinical specimens. The particles showed excellent affinity towards RNA with a dissociation constant of 2.32
×
10−8 M indicating a high tendency to adsorb RNA (Figs. 8S and 9S ) [39]. The emission enhancement of N-CDs/SiO2 is generally attributed to many factors [40], one of which is the crosslinker-enhanced emission (CEE). The inherent confined domain of CEE leads to fluorescence enhancement via different mechanisms including a) Suppressing the free rotation on N-CDs, which is known to favor both vibrational cascade (VC) and internal conversion (IC) radiationless deactivation pathways with concomitant decrease in fluorescence efficiency [41] and, b) Increasing the absorption cross section giving excitons that undergo migration and trapping at N-CDs leading to their enhanced fluorescence efficiencies [42–44]. Cationic N- CDs/SiO2/NH2 interaction with the HCV RNA anionic phosphate backbone, enhanced their direct electrostatic/ionic interaction, leading to an alignment of the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 onto the RNA phosphate backbone, reducing the particle's forceful collisions, besides the normal tertiary RNA folding structure that limit the distance between the interacted particles, providing rigid/aggregated/clustering structure as shown by AFM images (Fig. 8a and b) [15,45]. Moreover, Analysis of the positive and negative samples by fluorescence microscope revealed multiple bright fluorescence patches/spots in the positive samples (∼9–11 μm) compared to the negative samples as shown in (Fig. 8d and c) respectively. Interestingly, the time-resolved fluorescence decay (Fig. 8e) showed a significant increase in the fluorescence lifetime (τ 1 and τ 2 ) of the particles in the presence of RNA (τ1 = 1.96 ns and τ2 = 6.02 ns) compared to the particles with negative sample (τ1 = 1.49 ns and τ2 = 4.30 ns). This prolonged lifetime is in strong agreement with the literature in that surface cross-linking and interactions lead to an increase in the fluorescence lifetime [46,47].

These synergistic factors promote the CEE of the N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 by limiting/suppressing the nonradiative transitions (radiation-less decay such as vibrations and rotations), thereby imposing the excited electrons back to the ground state through radiative fluorescent emission, and hence, the emission enhanced drastically in the presence of the HCV RNA [15,48]. A comparison between the designed biosensors with selected biosensors for HCV RNA detection is shown in Table 1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Conclusion
ce:section.ce:para: The system introduced herein utilizes the fluorescent enhancement of the as-synthesized N-CDs/SiO2/NH2 as a tool to detect and quantify nucleic acids, exemplified by HCV RNA. Magnetic beads functionalized with specific HCV probe have been used for specific RNA extraction to enhance selectivity. This system was employed in both traditional 96 microwell plate format and on-chip microfluidic with pronounced advantages of the chip such as using a quarter of the reagents volume, proper mixing, resolving small concentrations, and enhancing the sensitivity and specificity to 96 % and 98 % respectively, with a limit of detection (LOD) of 500 IU/ml and 1000 IU/ml in the microfluidic chip and conventional 96 well plate respectively. The developed assay is highly sensitive, specific, simple, robust, cost cost-effective with a turnaround time of 15 min from sample to viral load. Furthermore, it could be fully automated, requires minimal user training, and is easily tailored towards other nucleic acid targets making it an accessible option for assay development and clinical applications.
